words.shortDefinition: Merge developer code changes into shared repositories multiple times daily via automated builds.
words.simpleDefinition: Automatically build and test code every time a developer commits changes — catching bugs early before they accumulate. It ensures the shared mainline remains in a stable, functional state for the entire team.
words.longDefinition: Continuous Integration (CI) automates the process of merging code changes from multiple contributors into a single software project. By running automated builds and tests on every commit, teams detect integration errors and regressions immediately. This frequency prevents 'integration hell' where massive merge conflicts occur at the end of a sprint, ensuring the codebase remains in a consistently deployable and functional state for the entire development team.
words.connector: Think of continuous integration like a collaborative digital document — changes sync instantly as you type, ensuring everyone sees the most recent, correct version.
words.techContext: Our CI pipeline runs the full suite of unit tests on every pull request to ensure no regressions are introduced.
words.exampleSentence: Adopting continuous integration reduced our merge conflict resolution time from hours to minutes by catching overlaps early.

codeExample.code:
on: [push]
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npm install && npm test

words.shortDefinition: Sequence of automated steps that compile, test, and deploy code to production.
words.simpleDefinition: An automated workflow that takes code from commit to production — performing builds, tests, and deployments in order. Each stage acts as a quality gate that must pass before the software advances.
words.longDefinition: A CI/CD pipeline is a series of automated steps that orchestrate the software delivery process. It typically includes stages for linting code, running unit and integration tests, building binary artifacts or Docker images, and deploying to staging or production environments. By standardizing the release process, pipelines increase deployment frequency, improve reliability, and provide a clear audit trail for every change entering the system.
words.connector: Think of a pipeline like an automated car wash — the vehicle enters one end and undergoes specific cleaning stages before emerging spotless.
words.techContext: The CI pipeline failed during the 'build' stage because of a missing dependency in the package.json file.
words.exampleSentence: Our deployment pipeline failed at the security scanning stage due to a high-severity vulnerability in a third-party dependency.

codeExample.code:
stages:
  - build
  - test
  - deploy

build_job:
  stage: build
  script: npm run build

words.etymology: Metaphor for a series of pipes that transport fluid (data/code) from one end to another.

words.shortDefinition: Logical grouping of pipeline jobs that must complete before the next set begins.
words.simpleDefinition: A high-level phase in a pipeline — like 'Build', 'Test', or 'Deploy' — that organizes related tasks. Stages typically act as sequential checkpoints that ensure the software meets specific quality standards before progressing further.
words.longDefinition: Stages provide a structural framework for CI/CD pipelines, allowing teams to organize jobs into logical phases. A standard pipeline might have a 'Build' stage for compilation, a 'Test' stage for quality checks, and a 'Deploy' stage for environment updates. Jobs within a stage usually run in parallel, but the entire stage must succeed before the pipeline moves to the next, preventing broken code from advancing.
words.connector: Think of stages like levels in a video game — you must complete all the challenges in one world before moving to the next.
words.techContext: The pipeline stopped because a job in the 'Test' stage failed, preventing the code from moving to the 'Deploy' stage.
words.exampleSentence: The deployment stalled because the 'Security Scan' stage discovered an unpatched library, blocking the pipeline from reaching the production stage.

codeExample.code:
stages:
  - build
  - security
  - deploy

words.etymology: from Latin stagium ('a place for standing').

words.shortDefinition: Static analysis of source code to identify programmatic errors and stylistic inconsistencies.
words.simpleDefinition: Automated checking of source code for potential bugs and stylistic errors — acting like an advanced spell-checker for programming. It ensures consistency across a codebase and catches common mistakes before the code is even compiled or run.
words.longDefinition: Linting is the first line of defense in code quality, involving tools that scan source code for patterns that might lead to bugs or maintainability issues. Linters enforce a uniform coding style, which is crucial for large teams working on the same repository. By catching syntax errors, unused variables, and security anti-patterns early in the CI pipeline, linting prevents simple mistakes from reaching later, more expensive testing stages.
words.connector: Think of linting like a grammar checker for your code — it catches typos and style issues before anyone else has to read it.
words.techContext: The CI job failed because the linter found three unused variables and a missing semicolon.
words.exampleSentence: Our CI pipeline rejected the pull request because the linting step failed on a trailing comma and an undeclared variable.
words.etymology: from 'lint' (tiny bits of fiber removed from clothes).

words.shortDefinition: Human-readable data serialization format used for configuring CI/CD pipelines and infrastructure states.
words.simpleDefinition: Human-readable format for writing configuration files — popular for defining CI/CD pipelines and cloud resources. It uses indentation to organize data hierarchies, making it significantly easier for developers to read and edit than older formats like XML or JSON.
words.longDefinition: YAML (YAML Ain't Markup Language) is the de facto standard for configuration in modern DevOps. Its clean, whitespace-sensitive syntax makes complex structures like Kubernetes deployments and GitHub Actions workflows easy to manage. Because it is highly readable and supports comments, it is ideal for version-controlled files where multiple developers need to understand the configuration logic. Most CI/CD tools interpret YAML files to determine how to build, test, and deploy applications.
words.connector: Think of YAML like a clean, indented outline for a book — the spacing tells you exactly which chapters belong to which sections.
words.techContext: I updated the .github/workflows/deploy.yml file to add a new step for running end-to-end tests.
words.exampleSentence: I had to fix a syntax error in the YAML file because I accidentally used tabs instead of spaces for indentation.

codeExample.code:
pipeline:
  name: My Build
  steps:
    - name: Run Tests
      command: npm test

words.etymology: Recursive acronym: YAML Ain't Markup Language.

words.shortDefinition: An event or schedule that causes a CI/CD pipeline to start.
words.simpleDefinition: The thing that kicks off a pipeline — a push, a pull-request, a tag, a cron schedule, or a manual click.
words.longDefinition: Triggers map source-of-truth events (git push, PR opened, tag created) to pipeline runs. Designing triggers well — only what should run, only when it should — keeps CI cost down and feedback loops fast.
words.techContext: GitHub Actions calls them `on:` events, GitLab calls them `rules:`, Jenkins uses webhook + cron triggers. The common failure mode is over-triggering — running the full E2E suite on every doc commit.
words.exampleSentence: Configure the deploy pipeline to trigger only on tags matching `v*.*.*`, not on every push to main.
words.etymology: From the firearm part that initiates the firing sequence.
words.connector: Like an event handler in code — `on(push) → run(pipeline)`.

codeExample.code:
# GitHub Actions
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    paths-ignore: ['docs/**']
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 2 * * *'

words.shortDefinition: Compiled binaries or packaged resources produced during the build stage of a pipeline.
words.simpleDefinition: The deployable outputs created by a build process — such as JAR files, Docker images, or minified JavaScript bundles. These immutable files are passed through subsequent pipeline stages for testing and deployment.
words.longDefinition: Build artifacts are the tangible results of a pipeline's compilation and packaging steps. They represent a specific version of the software that is ready to be tested or released. Storing these artifacts centrally allows teams to track exactly what version was deployed to which environment. Since artifacts are immutable, they ensure that the exact same code tested in staging is the one eventually running in production.
words.connector: Think of artifacts like the finished dishes in a kitchen — they are the actual items served to customers after the cooking is done.
words.techContext: The pipeline uploads the compiled .apk file as an artifact so the QA team can download and test the latest build on their devices.
words.exampleSentence: The CI runner uploaded the compiled Docker image as an artifact to the private registry for the deployment job to pull.

codeExample.code:
- name: Upload Build Artifact
  uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
  with:
    name: release-bundle
    path: dist/

words.etymology: from Latin arte ('by skill') + factum ('thing made').

words.shortDefinition: Automated HTTP callbacks triggered by events in one application to notify another.
words.simpleDefinition: Automated HTTP POST requests sent from one system to another when a specific event occurs — like a code push or payment. They enable real-time integration without the resource-heavy overhead of constant polling.
words.longDefinition: Webhooks provide a way for applications to send real-time data to other applications. Unlike APIs where you poll for data, webhooks are 'push' mechanisms triggered by events. When an event happens (e.g., a GitHub commit), the source site makes an HTTP request to the URL configured for the webhook. This is essential for CI/CD systems to start pipelines immediately upon detecting repository changes.
words.connector: Think of webhooks like a doorbell — instead of you checking the porch every minute, the visitor notifies you exactly when they arrive.
words.techContext: We set up a GitHub webhook to trigger our CI pipeline every time a developer pushes code to the repository.
words.exampleSentence: We configured a GitHub webhook to notify our Slack channel every time a production deployment succeeds or fails.

words.shortDefinition: Securely stored sensitive credentials used by automated pipelines to authenticate with external services.
words.simpleDefinition: Sensitive credentials — like API keys or database passwords — stored securely within a CI/CD platform and injected at runtime. They are protected from logs and hidden from unauthorized users to maintain environment security.
words.longDefinition: CI Secrets are a security feature of pipeline platforms that protect sensitive information. Instead of hardcoding credentials in configuration files, developers store them in a secure vault provided by the CI tool (e.g., GitHub Actions Secrets). These values are masked in logs and only exposed as environment variables during job execution. This practice prevents accidental leaks of production credentials while allowing pipelines to interact with cloud providers and databases.
words.connector: Think of CI secrets like a combination to a safe — only the authorized employee (the pipeline) knows it to get the work done.
words.techContext: I added the AWS_ACCESS_KEY to our GitHub Secrets so the pipeline can authenticate with our cloud provider and deploy the app.
words.exampleSentence: I moved the production database password to GitHub Secrets to ensure it wasn't committed to the source code as plain text.

codeExample.code:
steps:
  - name: Deploy
    env:
      DB_PASSWORD: ${{ secrets.DB_PROD_PASSWORD }}
    run: ./deploy.sh

words.shortDefinition: Automated testing to verify that new code changes haven't broken existing system functionality.
words.simpleDefinition: The automated process of re-running old tests to confirm that new code hasn't accidentally broken existing features. It acts as a safety net — preventing the software from 'regressing' into a buggier state during rapid development cycles.
words.longDefinition: Regression testing involves executing a suite of functional tests whenever code is updated to ensure that previously working features remain intact. In a CI/CD environment, this usually consists of unit, integration, and end-to-end tests that run on every pull request. By validating that new features don't introduce side effects in unrelated modules, regression testing gives teams the confidence to move fast and release software frequently without fear of breakage.
words.connector: Think of regression testing like a health checkup after surgery — you're ensuring the fix didn't cause new problems elsewhere in the body.
words.techContext: Our regression testing suite caught a bug in the checkout flow that was caused by a small change in the tax calculation library.
words.exampleSentence: The regression testing suite caught a critical failure in the login flow that was caused by a minor change in the cookie-parsing logic.

words.shortDefinition: Ensuring code is always in a releasable state for manual deployment at any time.
words.simpleDefinition: The practice of ensuring code is always in a releasable state, even if production updates aren't triggered automatically — emphasizing a robust pipeline. Every change is verified and ready for a final manual release approval by the team.
words.longDefinition: Continuous Delivery (CD) is the practice of automating the entire software release process up to the point of production deployment. While every change is automatically built, tested, and pushed to a staging environment, the final release to production requires a manual decision. This approach gives teams the benefits of high automation and fast feedback while allowing them to control the timing of releases based on business needs or marketing schedules.
words.connector: Think of continuous delivery like having a suitcase always packed by the door — you're ready to leave the moment you choose.
words.techContext: Our team uses continuous delivery, so we have a 'Deploy' button in GitLab that the product manager clicks after final verification.
words.exampleSentence: Our team uses continuous delivery to ensure the main branch is always production-ready, though we only push updates on Wednesday mornings.

codeExample.code:
deploy_prod:
  stage: deploy
  script: ./deploy.sh
  when: manual
  only:
    - main

words.shortDefinition: Automatically release every code change passing automated tests directly into production environments.
words.simpleDefinition: Automated workflow where every code change passing the test suite is immediately pushed to production — without manual intervention. This provides a lightning-fast feedback loop between development and real-world user behavior.
words.longDefinition: Continuous Deployment takes automation to the final stage by automatically pushing every verified change into production without human intervention. This practice eliminates manual release bottlenecks and enables a high-frequency update cycle. While it requires a mature and highly reliable automated testing suite to prevent shipping bugs, it allows teams to deliver value to users immediately and gather real-time performance data on new features.
words.connector: Think of continuous deployment like an automated vending machine — as soon as the item passes inspection, it is available for purchase.
words.techContext: With continuous deployment, our new features are live for users within 10 minutes of the code being merged into the main branch.
words.exampleSentence: With continuous deployment, our bug fixes are live for all users within minutes of the PR being merged.

codeExample.code:
deploy:
  runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  needs: [test]
  steps:
    - name: Deploy to Vercel
      run: vercel --token ${{ secrets.VERCEL_TOKEN }} --prod

words.shortDefinition: Manage and provision technical infrastructure through machine-readable definition files instead of manual tools.
words.simpleDefinition: Managing cloud resources through definition files — rather than manual interface clicks. IaC allows developers to version, test, and automate the creation of servers and networks, ensuring identical environments across the entire development lifecycle.
words.longDefinition: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) allows teams to treat their infrastructure like software. Using tools like Terraform or AWS CloudFormation, engineers define virtual machines, databases, and networking rules in configuration files. This eliminates manual configuration drift, enables rapid environment replication, and allows infrastructure changes to be peer-reviewed and tested just like application code. IaC is a cornerstone of modern DevOps, providing scalability and consistency that manual processes cannot match.
words.connector: Think of Infrastructure as Code like a 3D printer for networks — you feed in the digital blueprint and the machine builds the real thing.
words.techContext: We use IaC to ensure that our staging and production environments are identical, eliminating 'configuration drift'.
words.exampleSentence: By using Infrastructure as Code, we were able to replicate our entire production stack in a new region within thirty minutes.

words.shortDefinition: Deployment strategy that incrementally replaces old instances with new ones to maintain availability.
words.simpleDefinition: Strategy that replaces application instances one by one — ensuring the system always maintains enough capacity to handle traffic. This provides zero-downtime updates by keeping the majority of the fleet active while specific nodes are being upgraded.
words.longDefinition: A rolling update is a deployment pattern that minimizes downtime by incrementally updating subsets of an application's instances. Instead of taking the whole service offline, the orchestrator (like Kubernetes) shuts down an old pod, starts a new one, and waits for it to become healthy before proceeding to the next. This ensures that the application remains available to users throughout the entire update process, though multiple versions may coexist briefly.
words.connector: Think of a rolling update like replacing seats in a theater one by one during a show — the audience can still sit.
words.techContext: The rolling update replaced one pod at a time, ensuring that the total capacity of our service never dropped below 80%.
words.exampleSentence: The Kubernetes rolling update ensured that our user traffic was never interrupted while we deployed the latest security patches to the API.

codeExample.code:
strategy:
  type: RollingUpdate
  rollingUpdate:
    maxUnavailable: 25%
    maxSurge: 1

words.shortDefinition: Process or machine that executes the specific jobs defined in a CI/CD pipeline.
words.simpleDefinition: The application or virtual machine that executes the automated tasks defined in a pipeline — like running scripts or building containers. Runners listen for jobs, carry out the instructions, and report the success or failure.
words.longDefinition: Build runners (or agents) are the workhorses of any CI/CD platform. They are lightweight processes that poll the central CI server for pending jobs. Once a job is assigned, the runner checks out the source code, prepares the environment (often using Docker), and executes the defined build steps. Runners can be hosted by the provider (cloud-managed) or self-hosted on private infrastructure for better control and security.
words.connector: Think of a build runner like a specialized factory worker — they wait for a specific set of instructions and then perform the task.
words.techContext: We had to add more build runners because our PR queue was getting too long and developers were waiting an hour for their tests to run.
words.exampleSentence: We scaled our build runner pool to twenty instances to handle the surge in pull requests before the major release deadline.

codeExample.code:
./config.sh --url https://github.com/my-org/my-repo --token REG_TOKEN
./run.sh

words.etymology: from 'build' + 'runner' (one who carries out a task).

words.shortDefinition: Workflow prohibiting code merges into a branch until all automated tests successfully pass.
words.simpleDefinition: Developer workflow where code merges are strictly prohibited unless automated builds and tests succeed. It acts as a digital gatekeeper — ensuring the shared 'main' branch remains pristine and deployable at all times by blocking faulty commits.
words.longDefinition: A gated check-in is a quality control mechanism that prevents developers from merging code that breaks the build or fails tests. When a developer submits a change, the CI system creates a temporary environment to run the full pipeline. Only if every stage passes is the change actually merged into the target branch. This ensures that the mainline is always in a stable, releasable state and prevents 'broken builds' from halting team progress.
words.connector: Think of a gated check-in like a border checkpoint — you must have all your papers in order before being allowed into the country.
words.techContext: We implemented a gated check-in policy so that the 'main' branch is always in a buildable and deployable state.
words.exampleSentence: Our gated check-in policy prevented a developer from merging a change that would have broken the production build due to a missing dependency.

codeExample.code:
on: pull_request
jobs:
  verify:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - run: npm test

words.shortDefinition: Running multiple pipeline jobs or test suites simultaneously to reduce overall execution time.
words.simpleDefinition: Running multiple pipeline tasks or tests simultaneously across different runners or CPU cores — drastically reducing total execution time. It enables teams to receive faster feedback on large test suites by distributing the heavy workload across multiple machines.
words.longDefinition: Parallel execution is an optimization technique used to speed up CI/CD pipelines by running independent tasks at the same time. Instead of waiting for one job to finish before starting the next, a pipeline can distribute tasks—like running frontend and backend tests—across multiple runners. This drastically reduces the feedback loop for developers, allowing them to fix issues faster. Modern CI platforms make it easy to parallelize testing suites by splitting them into smaller batches.
words.connector: Think of parallel execution like adding more lanes to a toll booth — more cars can pass through at once, making the trip faster.
words.techContext: By enabling parallel execution for our test suite, we reduced our total CI time from 20 minutes down to just 5.
words.exampleSentence: We reduced our CI pipeline time from thirty minutes to five by enabling parallel execution for our end-to-end test suite.

codeExample.code:
test:
  parallelism: 4
  docker:
    - image: cimg/node:16
  steps:
    - run: npm test

words.etymology: from Greek parallēlos ('beside each other') + execution.

words.shortDefinition: Examining source code without execution to detect bugs, security flaws, or stylistic issues.
words.simpleDefinition: The automated evaluation of source code for security flaws and quality issues without actually running the program. It identifies potential vulnerabilities and 'code smells' early in the pipeline — long before the software reaches a live production environment.
words.longDefinition: Static analysis, or Static Application Security Testing (SAST), involves tools that analyze source code or compiled binaries to find defects. Because the code isn't executed, these tools can cover 100% of the codebase, identifying paths that might lead to memory leaks, null pointer exceptions, or SQL injections. Incorporating static analysis into a CI/CD pipeline ensures that security and maintainability standards are enforced automatically before the code is even ready for dynamic testing.
words.connector: Think of static analysis like a building inspector checking the structure and wiring of a house before anyone actually lives there.
words.techContext: The static analysis tool flagged a potential SQL injection vulnerability in our legacy database module.
words.exampleSentence: The static analysis tool blocked the build because it found a hardcoded API key in a configuration file.
words.etymology: from Greek statikos ('causing to stand') + analysis.

words.shortDefinition: Store and reuse intermediate build results to accelerate future pipeline executions.
words.simpleDefinition: Storage mechanism that saves intermediate build results — like downloaded libraries or compiled code — for reuse in future runs. It significantly speeds up the CI pipeline by skipping redundant tasks that haven't changed since the last execution.
words.longDefinition: A build cache is a productivity booster in CI/CD that prevents the pipeline from doing the same work twice. By storing 'expensive' items like package dependencies (e.g., node_modules) or compiled binaries in a persistent storage layer, subsequent builds can simply download these files if the source hasn't changed. This is especially effective for large projects where downloading dependencies or full recompilation could otherwise take ten minutes or more.
words.connector: Think of a build cache like keeping pre-chopped vegetables in the fridge — you don't start from scratch every time you cook.
words.techContext: I configured the CI build cache for our Maven project, which reduced the 'Install Dependencies' stage from 4 minutes to 20 seconds.
words.exampleSentence: Enabling the build cache for our Node.js project cut the installation step from five minutes to thirty seconds on most runs.

codeExample.code:
- uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: ~/.npm
    key: ${{ runner.os }}-node-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}

words.shortDefinition: Release strategy using two identical production environments to enable zero-downtime updates.
words.simpleDefinition: Deployment strategy utilizing two identical production environments to ensure zero-downtime updates — shifting traffic instantly from the old (Blue) to the new (Green). This allows for immediate rollbacks by switching traffic back if issues arise.
words.longDefinition: Blue/Green deployment reduces risk by maintaining two identical production environments. Only one (Blue) is live at a time. The new version is deployed and tested in the idle environment (Green). Once verified, the load balancer redirects all traffic to Green. If the new version fails, traffic can be instantly routed back to Blue, significantly reducing the 'Time to Recover' (TTR) from a faulty release.
words.connector: Think of blue/green deployment like a two-stage theater — one stage is live while the other is being set up for the next act.
words.techContext: We use blue/green deployments to ensure that we never have to take the site offline while we're updating the backend services.
words.exampleSentence: We used blue/green deployment to update our core API, allowing us to roll back instantly when we detected a database connection leak.

codeExample.code:
aws elbv2 modify-listener \
  --listener-arn $LISTENER_ARN \
  --default-actions Type=forward,TargetGroupArn=$GREEN_TG_ARN

words.etymology: Color-based metaphor for two distinct, identical environments.

words.shortDefinition: Roll out new versions to a small percentage of users before a full release.
words.simpleDefinition: Exposing a new software version to a tiny subset of real users — monitoring performance and error rates before a complete rollout. This strategy limits the 'blast radius' of potential bugs in production environments.
words.longDefinition: A canary release is a risk-reduction strategy where a new version of an application is incrementally introduced to the user base. Initially, only 1-5% of traffic is routed to the new version. If telemetry shows no increase in errors or latency, the percentage is gradually increased. This 'tasting' approach ensures that any unforeseen production issues only affect a small fraction of users, allowing for safe validation.
words.connector: Think of a canary release like a soft opening for a restaurant — only a few guests are invited to test the service first.
words.techContext: We released the new search algorithm to 5% of our users as a canary release to monitor its impact on server latency.
words.exampleSentence: The canary release detected a memory leak in the search service that only appeared under specific high-load conditions from real traffic.

codeExample.code:
http:
- route:
  - destination:
      host: my-service
      subset: v1
    weight: 90
  - destination:
      host: my-service
      subset: v2
    weight: 10

words.etymology: Metaphor for 'canaries in a coal mine' used to detect toxic gases before they affected the miners.

words.shortDefinition: Manage infrastructure and application state using Git as the single source of truth.
words.simpleDefinition: Operational framework using Git as the authoritative source for infrastructure and application configurations — where changes are applied via pull requests. Automated agents constantly synchronize the live environment with the state defined in Git.
words.longDefinition: GitOps extends DevOps practices to infrastructure management by treating configuration as code. In a GitOps workflow, the desired state of a system (like a Kubernetes host) is described in Git. An 'operator' or controller monitors the repository and automatically applies changes to the environment when it detects a diff. This provides a clear audit trail, simplifies rollbacks via 'git revert,' and ensures that manual changes are automatically overwritten.
words.connector: Think of GitOps like a restaurant's digital order board — the staff (automation) work to make the kitchen match exactly what's on the screen.
words.techContext: With GitOps, we deploy to production just by merging a YAML file change into the main branch of our infrastructure repo.
words.exampleSentence: We implemented GitOps using ArgoCD to ensure our staging and production clusters are always in sync with our infrastructure repository.

codeExample.code:
spec:
  source:
    repoURL: 'https://github.com/org/infra'
    targetRevision: HEAD
  destination:
    server: 'https://kubernetes.default.svc'

words.shortDefinition: Pipeline execution environment managed on internal infrastructure instead of a cloud provider's fleet.
words.simpleDefinition: A build runner server managed on your own infrastructure — rather than by a cloud provider. These are used for higher performance, custom software requirements, or to securely access resources within a private network that are not exposed publicly.
words.longDefinition: Self-hosted runners provide organizations with full control over the build environment. Unlike cloud-hosted runners that are ephemeral and standardized, self-hosted runners can be customized with specific hardware (like GPUs), pre-installed software, or persistent caches to speed up builds. They are also essential for security-conscious teams who need their CI/CD pipeline to interact with internal servers or databases that are restricted from the public internet by firewalls.
words.connector: Think of a self-hosted runner like having your own workshop instead of renting one — you can customize all the tools yourself.
words.techContext: We used a self-hosted runner to allow our CI pipeline to deploy code to our internal servers that are behind a firewall.
words.exampleSentence: We deployed a self-hosted runner in our private VPC so the pipeline could run database migrations against our internal RDS instance.

codeExample.code:
docker run -d --name my-runner \
  -e REPO_URL="https://github.com/org/repo" \
  -e ACCESS_TOKEN="xyz" \
  my-org/runner-image:latest

words.shortDefinition: Automated coordination of complex tasks required to release software across multiple environments.
words.simpleDefinition: Automated coordination of complex tasks required to release software across multiple environments — managing the precise timing and sequence of activities. It orchestrates database migrations and container rollouts to ensure a smooth, error-free production transition.
words.longDefinition: Deployment orchestration involves managing the end-to-end process of delivering software to various environments. It goes beyond simple automation by coordinating dependencies between multiple services, databases, and infrastructure components. For instance, an orchestrator might ensure that a database schema is updated before new application code is deployed. This high-level coordination reduces manual errors, ensures repeatable releases, and provides centralized visibility into the status of deployments across the entire organization.
words.connector: Think of deployment orchestration like a wedding planner — coordinating the florist, caterer, and band so everything happens in the perfect order.
words.techContext: We use AWS CodePipeline for deployment orchestration to manage the flow of code from GitHub to our production EKS cluster.
words.exampleSentence: We used deployment orchestration to sync the release of our mobile app update with the rollout of the new backend API.

words.shortDefinition: Maximum potential impact that a failure in a specific component has on the system.
words.simpleDefinition: The maximum potential impact an application failure or bad deployment can have on users and infrastructure. Modern deployment strategies like canary releases aim to minimize this 'radius' — ensuring that errors affect the smallest possible number of people before being caught.
words.longDefinition: Blast radius refers to the scope of damage that occurs when something goes wrong in a technical system. In DevOps, architects design systems to have a small blast radius by using microservices, feature flags, and incremental rollouts. If a new deployment has a critical bug, a small blast radius ensures that only a tiny fraction of users or a single non-essential service is affected, rather than causing a total platform outage that impacts all customers.
words.connector: Think of a blast radius like a power outage — if only one house loses power, the rest of the neighborhood stays lit.
words.techContext: We moved our payment gateway into a separate service to limit the blast radius of any potential deployment failures.
words.exampleSentence: We used a canary deployment to limit the blast radius of the new recommendation engine, protecting 95% of our users from potential errors.
words.etymology: Metaphor from military/explosives: the area affected by an explosion.
